methods.description: Methodology for processing surface sediment samples to analyze benthic foraminiferal communities, including sample preparation, identification, quantification, and subsequent statistical analysis.

steps.description: All 73 surface sediment samples for foraminiferal analysis were soaked and thoroughly mixed with a methanol and Rose Bengal solution (1.0 g/L) for the purpose of tinting living individuals to distinguish them from dead ones immediately after collection on board.
steps.searchable_summary: Soak surface sediment samples with methanol and Rose Bengal solution to stain living foraminifera

steps.description: These samples were dried and weighed...
steps.searchable_summary: Dry and weigh soaked sediment samples

steps.description: ...and then were wetsieved through a 63 mm-sieve in the lab.
steps.searchable_summary: Wet sieve dried sediment samples using a 63 µm sieve

steps.description: The coarse fraction (>63 mm) was dried and split...
steps.searchable_summary: Dry and split the coarse sediment fraction (>63 µm)

steps.description: ...and the split sample was examined completely for foraminiferal compositions. Benthic species were identified and quantified under a stereomicroscope with continuous zooming up to a maximum amplification of 40×. Living specimens were identified by the presence of Rose Bengal-stained protoplasm in their tests...Taxonomic identification mainly followed the protocol outlined by Zheng (1988); Wang et al. (1988), and Lei and Li (2016).
steps.searchable_summary: Examine split coarse sediment fraction under stereomicroscope to identify and quantify benthic foraminifera, distinguishing living individuals by Rose Bengal stain, following specified taxonomic protocols
inputs: Taxonomic protocols (Zheng 1988, Wang et al. 1988, Lei and Li 2016)
outputs: List of identified benthic foraminiferal species per sample
Counts of living foraminiferal individuals per species per sample
Counts of dead foraminiferal individuals per species per sample

steps.description: Faunal abundance (N, individuals ind./g dry weight of sediment) and foraminiferal species richness (H′, Shannon-Wiener diversity index based on calculation of equation (1), Shannon, 1984) were calculated for living and dead foraminiferal communities on raw data.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate faunal abundance (individuals per gram dry weight) and Shannon-Wiener diversity index for living and dead foraminiferal communities
inputs: Foraminiferal counts (living and dead) per species per sample (from step 5)
Dry weight of sediment per sample (from step 2)
outputs: Faunal abundance (N) for living community per sample
Faunal abundance (N) for dead community per sample
Shannon-Wiener diversity index (H') for living community per sample
Shannon-Wiener diversity index (H') for dead community per sample

steps.description: Benthic species with relative abundances of >5% in at least three samples were regarded as common species in the study coastal sea (Wang et al., 1988).
steps.searchable_summary: Identify common benthic foraminiferal species based on a relative abundance threshold (>5% in >=3 samples)
inputs: Relative abundances of benthic foraminiferal species per sample
outputs: List of common benthic foraminiferal species

steps.description: Additionally, we quantitatively interpreted the living and dead foraminiferal ecology by utilizing multivariate statistical methods based on ordination and classification methods such as Q-mode Hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA). HCA was used to classify the distribution of foraminiferal groups and subgroups into homogeneous assemblage zones.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Q-mode Hierarchical Clustering Analysis (HCA) on foraminiferal composition data to identify assemblage zones
inputs: Foraminiferal composition data (e.g., species counts or relative abundances) for living and/or dead communities

steps.description: RDA was applied to quantify the relationships between faunal structures and environmental variables.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Redundancy Analysis (RDA) to quantify relationships between foraminiferal community structure and environmental variables
inputs: Foraminiferal community structure data (e.g., species matrix)
outputs: Results of RDA (e.g., ordination plot, variance explained, species-environment correlations)
